risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "20 consecutive patients ... were included in the study and randomised to two groups (levosimendan and control groups)." Comment: The paper states that patients were randomized, but it does not describe how the allocation sequence was generated. There is no mention of a random number table, computer-generated sequence, coin toss, shuffled envelopes/cards, or other truly random method, so the sequence generation cannot be judged as truly random.

Summary: The report says the 20 consecutive patients were "randomised to two groups" but gives no description of how the allocation sequence was concealed from the person enrolling participants. There is no mention of central allocation, sealed opaque sequentially numbered envelopes, identical containers, or any other mechanism preventing foreknowledge of the next assignment. Comment: Randomisation alone does not establish concealment, so this domain cannot be judged low risk.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Summary: The report states that patients were randomized to levosimendan or control and that "The anaesthetic and surgical management of all patients was the same in both groups," but it does not say whether participants, surgeons, anesthetists, or other key personnel were blinded, nor whether a placebo was used. Comment: Because the paper provides no explicit blinding information for participants/personnel, blinding status cannot be determined confidently; although the outcomes are haemodynamic measures, the report is insufficient to judge whether knowledge of allocation could have affected care or outcome measurement.

Summary: The outcomes were measured by invasive haemodynamic monitoring with a 7F thermodilution catheter and a computer system, with values recorded at prespecified time points. "Measurements were performed using a 7F Multiflex thermodilution catheter... Five measurements were carried out for each parameter... averages... were obtained. On the other hand, PVR and SVR values were calculated and recorded by the computer system." Comment: These are objective, instrument-based haemodynamic outcomes that are unlikely to be influenced by whether the assessor knew the allocation, so lack of explicit blinding of the outcome assessor is unlikely to introduce detection bias.

Quote: "N lost to follow‐up or withdrawn:0" and Table 1 / Table 3 report outcomes for Group I (n = 10) and Group II (n = 10), with no indication of participant loss or exclusion after randomisation. Comment: Follow-up appears complete for all 20 randomised participants, and the paper reports analyses on the full randomized sample without any signal of differential dropout or missing outcome data that could bias the results.
